video_title: Why Coding is The First Undeniable AI Use case | a16z
gist: Benedict Evans argues coding is the only AI use case with undeniable product-market fit so far, while foundation models look set to become low-margin commodity infrastructure (like telecom networks) where the value migrates up the stack. He frames the current supply/demand/price imbalance in AI compute as transitory and stresses that the most important questions about AI's impact have moved outside of tech into industry-specific domains like law, consulting, finance, and advertising.

themes.theme: Coding as the only proven AI use case

insights.claim: Agentic coding shifted from 'kind of useful' to 'really changing everything' at the beginning of 2026, and is currently the only AI application with undeniable product-market fit where customers pull it out of vendors' hands.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Coding-focused AI tools and the labs serving them (e.g. Anthropic) have demonstrable demand; investors should treat coding PMF as proven while other verticals remain speculative.
insights.quote: clearly agentic coding started working and so all the focus in tech has kind of narrowed in massively onto that as something that has absolute product market fit in the sense that like the customers are pulling it out of your hands

insights.claim: That software developers built AI coding tools first was deterministically predictable because developers experiment with new tech on their own work, just as the first thing people did with PCs was build more computers and LLMs are themselves computers used to make more compute.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Use the 'builders automate their own work first' heuristic to anticipate which adjacent professional domains adopt AI next.
insights.quote: the first thing that people did with PCs was make computers... the first thing that people are doing with LLMs, in a sense, LLMs are computers, is to make more compute

insights.claim: The career structure and team organization of software engineering in three years is genuinely unknowable, and nobody can yet say what market structure or the role of junior engineers will look like.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Avoid overconfident bets on how AI reshapes engineering org structures; the question of why firms hired juniors (to do work vs. to train) is now live, not theoretical.
insights.quote: I don't think anybody can possibly say they kind of know what the market structure is going to look like or what the career of a software engineer is going to be in three years time

insights.claim: Coding-related software run-rate referenced as evidence of PMF jumped from roughly a $9 billion run rate at the end of last year to a $47 billion run rate, all of it software.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The scale of revenue growth concentrated in coding underscores how lopsided AI monetization currently is toward a single vertical.
insights.quote: it's gone from whatever it was 9 billion run rate at the end of last year to $47 billion run rate That's all software, isn't it?

insights.claim: Foundation models are likely to become commoditized low-level infrastructure because there is no apparent network effect or sustainable differentiation between models beyond willingness to spend, while a chatbot is a limited v1 UI rather than a finished product.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: If models commoditize, value and margin accrue to the application/tooling layer, not the labs; weigh this against the record capital flowing into foundation model companies.
insights.quote: I don't think foundation models are a product. I don't think a chatbot is a product. I think the value will be further up.

insights.claim: Foundation models resemble hyperscalers/cloud rather than operating systems: customers don't standardize their SaaS purchases on a specific model the way they never picked SaaS by which cloud it runs on, so labs lack leverage and control over what gets built.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Lab-level lock-in is unlikely; bet on the abstraction of model choice away from the buyer, favoring application vendors over model providers for pricing power.
insights.quote: you didn't say well you know our company standardized on AWS like you don't even know what company what what cloud that that that SAS product one is on that's the whole point it's abstracted away

insights.claim: Foundation models may follow a semiconductor-like consolidation path where each generation gets more expensive and fewer players can afford to compete.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Watch for the field narrowing to a handful of frontier labs as model-training costs escalate, mirroring the semiconductor capex curve.
insights.quote: maybe the right comparison here is with semiconductors where with each generation it just gets more expensive and so you have fewer players

insights.claim: With three to six companies building frontier models and spending somewhere between $200 billion and $2 trillion a year, plus edge and open-source models, there is no obvious source of price discipline and thus little pricing power.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Expect a 'murderous price war' in tokens akin to telecom data; developers will swap models freely, compressing lab margins.
insights.quote: where is the price discipline going to come from? Particularly when some of them have got like whole other business models as well like... Google selling ads

insights.claim: Evans frames his commodity thesis as a falsifiable chain of argument rather than a certainty, inviting counterarguments for why models won't commoditize.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Treat the commodity thesis as a base case to stress-test, not a prediction; the bull case requires identifying a durable model moat or up-stack leverage.
insights.quote: here is a chain of argument that says that deterministically it looks like these things will be commodities and explain to me why they won't be

insights.claim: Mobile operators built sophisticated global infrastructure and saw data traffic rise roughly 1,500-2,000x over 15 years, yet captured almost none of the value because all the cool stuff was built by others and their stocks stayed flat for 20 years.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A cautionary template for AI labs: massive usage growth and indispensable infrastructure do not guarantee value capture or shareholder returns.
insights.quote: they built this amazing piece of global incredibly sophisticated very expensive global infrastructure with enormous growth in use all the time and it changed all of our lives and we all pay for it and they didn't make any money from it because all the value moved up stack

insights.claim: The current AI token pricing crunch (e.g. paying $20/month for ~$10k of tokens, or running up a $10k bill in days) mirrors the mobile-data bill-shock of 2009-2010 and is a transitory disequilibrium that will resolve into a normal supply/demand price equilibrium.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate today's token economics; expect bundles, fair-use, and throttling-style repricing as costs align with value, just as carriers did.
insights.quote: on the one hand you're paying $20 a month and you get 10 grand worth of tokens and on the other hand like... you messed about for a couple of days and you get a bill for 10 grand

insights.claim: Infinite demand for tokens does not prevent a low-price equilibrium, just as infinite demand for mobile bits coexisted with a brutal telecom price war because both are swappable commodities.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Rebuts the bull argument that limitless token demand guarantees lab profitability; commodity dynamics can still drive prices to marginal cost.
insights.quote: just because demand for tokens is infinite that doesn't mean that you can't get to a different price equilibrium because of course that's what happened with mobile data

insights.claim: Big Tech (Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Apple) almost certainly produces more profit than the entire global telecoms industry, illustrating that infrastructure builders historically lose to the value layer above them.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Reinforces the case for owning the up-stack value layer rather than the AI infrastructure layer.
insights.quote: you're a pretty safe bet that Google, Meta, Amazon, um Microsoft, Apple produce more profits than the entire telecoms industry

themes.theme: AI capex limits and financial gravity

insights.claim: Microsoft, Meta and Google are all on track to spend over 50% of revenue on capex this year, versus telecoms historically spending only 15-20% of revenue on capex.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: AI capex intensity dwarfs even the famously capital-heavy telecom sector, a red flag for sustainability of spending versus free cash flow.
insights.quote: Microsoft, Meta and Google are all on in line to spend over 50% of revenue on capex this year... telecom spend instead of 15 to 20% of revenue on capex

insights.claim: Big-four AI capex guidance of ~$700 billion this year is comparable to total telecoms (~$300B) and oil and gas ($700B-$1T) capex, so it is large but not impossible; however $1.5 trillion next year would require borrowing and could not be sustained.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: There is a hard 'financial gravity' ceiling on AI capex growth; spending cannot scale indefinitely and must taper, regardless of FOMO.
insights.quote: $700 billion a year is not an impossibly large amount of money as of what big global infrastructure costs. It's just a lot of money.

insights.claim: There are 'laws of physics' caps on AI infrastructure spend because the money simply isn't there to spend $10 trillion a year, so the capex growth curve must eventually slow.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Models a finite top to the AI infrastructure trade; the deceleration point matters for chip and data-center demand forecasts.
insights.quote: we can't spend $10 trillion a year on inf AI infrastructure because there isn't $10 trillion a year there to spend on it

insights.claim: AI capex is an existential FOMO problem for hyperscalers: current returns are hugely positive and they cannot let rivals build the future of compute without participating, even as CFOs question how much participation is warranted.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Explains why capex stays high despite uncertain ROI; the risk being hedged is becoming the next IBM-in-the-90s or Microsoft-in-the-2000s, not near-term return.
insights.quote: you can't let other people get away with this without you participating because then your company's gone

insights.claim: Most measured AI benefits in CFO surveys (Deloitte and a Fed survey cited) so far are hard-to-quantify gains like better analytics, customer support, and faster slide/analysis production, rather than new revenue lines or clear cost savings.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Near-term AI ROI is soft and productivity-flavored; investors should expect a lag before measurable revenue or cost impact shows up in financials.
insights.quote: most of the benefits so far have been stuff that's pretty hard to measure so like better analytics better customer support

insights.claim: AI productivity gains may get competed away as consumer surplus: like Excel, if a task that took a week now takes a day, professionals just do far more of it at the same price with the same cost base rather than capturing new margin.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Caution against assuming AI efficiency translates into expanded margins for professional-services firms; competitive necessity erodes the gain.
insights.quote: if a DCF takes you a week, then you probably only do one or two DCFs. And if a DCF takes you 10 seconds, then you do 50 DCFs, but you probably can't charge any more money for that

insights.claim: A token-maxing reckoning is likely as the disequilibrium resolves: pricing must realign with cost, usage with pricing, and pricing with ROI, similar to the mobile bill-shock correction of 2010.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Expect enterprises to pull back on premium-model overuse once ROI studies mature, pressuring per-token revenue assumptions.
insights.quote: the pricing has got to get back into alignment with the cost and the usage has got to get it into alignment with the pricing and the ROI

themes.theme: The future of software and the SaaS apocalypse

insights.claim: AI will produce far more software, not less, because cheaper/faster building lowers barriers and all software exists to solve problems created by other software, mirroring how SaaS produced one to two orders of magnitude more software.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Bet on software proliferation and more competition rather than consolidation; the 'AI replaces software' narrative is likely backwards.
insights.quote: what does this do to software and the answer is more software like way more software

insights.claim: The 'SaaS apocalypse' is real but unspecific: some percentage of existing SaaS companies will be wiped out by AI, but since nobody knows which ones, derating the whole sector by 50% is wrong even though Evans wouldn't be long software right now.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Stock-picking matters more than a blanket sector short; the uncertainty itself is a reason for caution on software longs near-term.
insights.quote: I'm not sure I'm going to be long software at the moment until I have some idea of what the hell's going on

insights.claim: The key software design question is whether the LLM sits at the bottom of the stack (a controlled feature inside Salesforce with guardrails) or the top (synthesizing across many systems), and the answer is probably both depending on the task.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A lens for evaluating AI software startups: where they place probabilistic vs. deterministic software determines reliability and defensibility.
insights.quote: where do you put the probabilistic software that can make mistakes and where do you put the deterministic system software that can't

insights.claim: New AI pricing models like outcome-based pricing face a structural problem because most enterprise software actions can't be cleanly tied to P&L, unlike specific cases in Salesforce.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Skepticism toward outcome-based AI pricing as a universal model; per-seat or usage pricing likely persists where value attribution is fuzzy.
insights.quote: it's really hard to like tie each button press in a piece of enterprise software to P&L

insights.claim: Rebuilding existing products with new technology (e.g. Google Docs rebuilding Office on the web) is a recurring fallacy; Google Docs only captured ~20% of the market because the value is in doing something new, not replicating the old.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Discount 'rebuild X with AI' pitches; the durable winners create capabilities that were previously impossible rather than re-platforming incumbents.
insights.quote: Look at Google Docs. It's got like 20% of the market because that's actually not the point. What's interesting is to do something else, is to do something new.

themes.theme: Where AI questions move outside of tech

insights.claim: The most consequential AI questions have moved out of technology into industry-specific domains (law, consulting, finance, advertising), much as Netflix's key questions became Los Angeles media questions and Tesla's became car-company questions.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Domain expertise, not AI expertise, will determine who captures value in each vertical; evaluate AI vertical plays on industry insight.
insights.quote: what does this stuff mean for law is kind of a question for lawyers as much as it is or people who understand a lot about law firms

insights.claim: Professional-services pyramids (law firms, consultancies, investment banks) face restructuring as AI automates much of what bottom-of-pyramid associates do, but outsiders can't predict the outcome without knowing what associates actually do and what clients actually pay for.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The margin and hiring structure of professional services is a major AI battleground; insider knowledge is required to handicap winners.

insights.claim: AI could transform the trillion-dollar advertising and $25 trillion retail markets because LLMs can understand what products actually are and why people buy them, going beyond Google/Meta/Amazon's current SKU-and-metadata-level correlations.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Watch for AI-driven step-changes in ad conversion and product understanding; commerce/ad-tech is a large addressable opportunity beyond coding.
insights.quote: advertising is a trillion dollars and retail is $25 trillion so you know is a reasonable sized tam

insights.claim: Surging ad revenue and conversion rates at Google and Facebook each quarter are being driven by rolling LLM-grade understanding into their recommendation, prediction, and ad-targeting systems.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Already-deployed AI in ad systems is a concrete, measurable monetization channel; ad-platform revenue acceleration is a near-term AI signal.
insights.quote: they have these enormous this sudden acceleration in their ad revenue and all of which is is to say like you kind of look at um how these systems work

insights.claim: OpenAI pursued an 'everything all at once' strategy (asking ChatGPT for 15 ideas to build value on infrastructure and doing all of them), while Anthropic, with less capital raised, deliberately or by stumbling focused on coding and got it working.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Focus beat breadth in capturing the one proven AI vertical; capital constraints may have driven Anthropic's winning narrowness.
insights.quote: Anthropic with having less capital raised said, "No, we're going to focus on coding." And they got coding working.

insights.claim: Fiji Simo taking a medical leave shuffled OpenAI's product organization amid its strategy iterations.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Leadership disruption at OpenAI is a factor in its product-strategy volatility worth tracking.

insights.claim: A roughly 60/40 adoption split exists between Valley power users running clusters of Mac Studios on AI all day and the ~40% who find it merely 'kind of useful' and used it once last week, with coding the only domain that has bridged that gap.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The engagement chasm between enthusiasts and casual users signals that daily-active consumer AI beyond coding is unproven.
insights.quote: those other 40% of people who say yeah it's kind of useful um I used it last week for something

themes.theme: Adoption dynamics and historical platform analogies

insights.claim: AI's massive weekly-active-user numbers (e.g. ~900 million) reflect compounding adoption on prior platforms, since unlike Netscape's era (only double-digit-millions of PCs) the installed base of devices and internet users already exists.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: AI's fast top-of-funnel growth is partly inherited from existing infrastructure; weekly vs. daily engagement is the more meaningful metric.
insights.quote: when your boss my old boss Mark Andre was working on Netscape there were like doubledigit millions of PCs on the entire planet

insights.claim: AI differs from prior platform shifts in one critical way: with 3G, the iPhone or the web you knew the physical limits, but with generative AI we don't know how much bigger, better, faster or cheaper the models will get.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The absence of known physical ceilings makes AI forecasting fundamentally harder; scenario-planning beats point predictions.
insights.quote: with generative AI, obviously, we don't like... we don't know those kinds of questions. So, how much bigger will the models get? How much better? How much faster? How much cheaper?

insights.claim: All comparisons to prior tech shifts are useful but none are predictive, illustrated by how many clever people in tech wrongly predicted Android's openness would crush the iPhone.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Resist 'it worked like that last three times' reasoning when handicapping AI outcomes; historical patterns prompt questions, not answers.
insights.quote: all of these comparisons are useful none of them are predictive

insights.claim: A useful framework for AI's economic impact is four 'buttons': price elasticity (Jevons paradox), removing cost barriers to entry, unlocking new business models, and enabling previously impossible things (like steam engines enabling trains or Spotify's all-you-can-eat $15/month model).
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A structured lens for evaluating which AI applications create genuinely new value versus merely doing the old thing cheaper.
insights.quote: the important stuff is not doing the old thing but more. It's doing something new that you couldn't have done with the old thing.

themes.theme: Why the model won't do the whole thing

insights.claim: The model can't 'do the whole thing' because many valuable products solve problems that nobody in the industry even knew existed, requiring founders to discover and explain latent problems rather than middle managers prompting a model.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Supports an applications-layer investment thesis: value comes from problem discovery and product design that labs cannot internalize.
insights.quote: no one including the people who have that problem no one realized that problem existed and then they'll go out and make a thing to solve it

insights.claim: Much of what happens inside organizations is implicit, undocumented, and absent from training data, which is precisely the value of consultancies like Bain, BCG and McKinsey that gain license to discover how a company actually works versus how it's supposed to.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Tacit organizational knowledge is a moat against full AI automation of strategy and operations work, protecting human-led consulting value.
insights.quote: how much of what's done inside an organization is implicit and not documented and not in the training data

insights.claim: LLMs excel where you want 'the average'—the answer anyone would give—and are weak where you want a new or different answer that can't be explained as how people normally do it.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A test for AI automatability: tasks demanding consensus/average outputs are exposed, while differentiated judgment work is defensible.
insights.quote: LLMs are are going to be very good at anything where you can describe how people do it and where what you want is the way anybody would do that and not so good at where you can't really explain why you did it like that

insights.claim: The distinction between a 'task' and a 'job' explains AI's impact: tasks change completely (accountants do almost nothing the same as 50 years ago) while the job sold to the client stays largely the same.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Automation of tasks doesn't necessarily destroy the job or the client relationship; analyze AI disruption at the task level, not the job level.
insights.quote: if you think about what accountants did 50 years ago and what accountants do today... they do spend almost none of their time doing the same things but like to the client it's kind of the same thing

themes.theme: Edge, open source, and on-device models

insights.claim: A key open question is when classes of use cases become 'good enough' to run on older, open-source, or on-device models where compute is effectively free to the developer, with Apple expected to address on-device AI imminently.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: On-device and open-source models could erode cloud frontier-model demand for many tasks, undermining lab pricing power; watch Apple's announcements.
insights.quote: how much can you push onto the device where the compute is free or free to you anyway doesn't have marginal cost for the developer

themes.theme: Long-run framing: AI becomes invisible magic

insights.claim: AI should be treated as another roughly-decadal platform shift like PCs, internet and mobile that will create great new things, ruin some lives and displace workers, and in 20 years become invisible 'magic' we take for granted.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A base-case mental model that tempers both AI hype and doom; the durable lesson is that transformative tech eventually normalizes and its value migrates unpredictably.
insights.quote: in 20 years time we'll just say, well, of course that's how it is. Computers have always done that.
